advantages: H(X) provides a single, interpretable number for average uncertainty—comparable across distributions with the same support size.
Shannon entropy is grounded in optimal lossless compression: no code can beat H(X) bits per symbol on average for source X.
Information gain from entropy reductions gives a principled, model-free criterion for decision-tree splits and feature relevance.
Entropy connects naturally to cross-entropy and KL divergence, forming the backbone of classification loss and distribution comparison in ML.
disadvantages: H(X) alone ignores tail behavior and misclassification costs—a low-entropy label set can still have costly rare errors.
Entropy is defined for discrete PMFs; continuous differential entropy can be negative and lacks the same 0-to-log n interpretation.
Computing H(X) from data requires estimating probabilities; small samples make plug-in entropy estimates biased and high-variance.
Maximizing information gain favors features with many levels (high cardinality), which can overfit unless corrected (e.g. gain ratio).
